verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: But now, since my pleading has steered its course out of these rough and broken places, and I have refuted the charge of heresy which had been urged against me by looking my accuser freely in the face, I will pass on to the other articles of charge with which he tries to assail me.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: The first is that I am a scurrilous person, a detractor of every one; that I am always snarling and biting at my predecessors.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: I ask him to name a single person whose reputation I have disparaged, or whom, according to an art practised by my opponent, I have galled by pretended praise.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: But, if I speak against ill-disposed persons, and wound with the point of my pen some Luscius Lanuvinus or an Asinius Pollio of the race of the Cornelii, if I repel the attacks of a man of boastful and curious spirit, and aim all my shafts at a single butt, why does he divide with others the wounds meant for him alone?

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: And why is he so unwise as to shew, by the irritation of his answer to my attack, his consciousness that it is he alone whom the cap fits?

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: He brings against me the charge of perjury and sacrilege together, because, in a book written for the instruction of one of Christ’s virgins, I describe the promise which I once made when I dreamed that I was before the tribunal of the Judge, that I would never again pay attention to secular literature, and that nevertheless I have sometimes made mention of the learning which I then condemned.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: I think that I have here lighted on the man who, under the name of Sallustianus Calpurnius, and through the letter written to me by the orator Magnus, raised a not very great question.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: My answer on the general subject is contained in the short treatise which I then wrote to him.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: But at the present moment I must make answer as to the sacrilege and perjury of my dream.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: I said that I would thenceforward read no secular books: it was a promise for the future, not the abolition of my memory of the past.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: How, you may ask me, can you retain what you have been so long without reading?

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: I must give my answer by recurring to one of these old books:

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: ’Tis much to be inured in tender youth.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: But by this mode of denial I criminate myself; for bringing Virgil as my witness I am accused by my own defender.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: I suppose I must weave a long web of words to prove what each man is conscious of.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Which of us does not remember his infancy?

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: I shall make you laugh though you are a man of such extreme gravity; and you will have at last to do as Crassus did, who, Lucilius tells us, laughed but once in his life, if I recount the memories of my childhood: how I ran about among the offices where the slaves worked; how I spent the holidays in play; or how I had to be dragged like a captive from my grandmother’s lap to the lessons of my enraged Orbilius.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: You may still more be astonished if I say that, even now that my head is gray and bald, I often seem in my dreams to be standing, a curly youth, dressed in my toga, to declaim a controversial thesis before the master of rhetoric; and, when I wake, I congratulate myself on escaping the peril of making a speech.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Believe me, our infancy brings back to us many things most accurately.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: If you had had a literary education, your mind would retain what it was originally imbued with as a wine cask retains its scent.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: The purple dye on the wool cannot be washed out with water.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Even asses and other brutes know the inns they have stopped at before, however long the journey may have been.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Are you astonished that I have not forgotten my Latin books when you learnt Greek without a master?

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: I learned the seven forms of Syllogisms in the Elements of logic; I learned the meaning of an Axiom, or as it might be called in Latin a Determination; I learned how every sentence must have in it a verb and a noun; how to heap up the steps of the Sorites, how to detect the clever turns of the Pseudomenos and the frauds of the stock sophisms.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: I can swear that I never read any of these things after I left school.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: I suppose that, to escape from having what I learned made into a crime, I must, according to the fables of the poets, go and drink of the river Lethe.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: I summon you, who accuse me for my scanty knowledge, and who think yourself a litérateur and a Rabbi, tell me how was it that you dared to write some of the things you have written, and to translate Gregory, that most eloquent man, with a splendour of eloquence like his own?

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Whence have you obtained that flow of words, that lucidity of statement, that variety of translations,—you who in youth had hardly more than a first taste of rhetoric?

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: I must be very much mistaken if you do not study Cicero in secret.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: I suspect that, being yourself so cultivated a person, you forbid me under penalties the reading of Cicero, so that you may be left alone among our church writers to boast of your flow of eloquence.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: I must say, however, that you seem rather to follow the philosophers, for your style is akin to that of the thorny sentences of Cleanthes and the contortions of Chrysippus, not from any art, for of that you say you are ignorant, but from the sympathy of genius.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: The Stoics claim Logic as their own, a science which you despise as a piece of fatuity; on this side, therefore, you are an Epicurean, and the principle of your eloquence is, not style but matter.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: For, indeed, what does it matter that no one else understands what you wish to say, when you write for your own friends alone, not for all?

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: I must confess that I myself do not always understand what you write, and think that I am reading Heraclitus; however I do not complain, nor lament for my sluggishness; for the trouble of reading what you write is not more than the trouble you must have in writing it.
